blocks.text: Chapter 2.
Anchors and Arbitrary Coherence: The Fallacy of Supply and Demand
Second night with Ariely, friend — and it begins with baby geese.

The ethologist Konrad Lorenz discovered that a gosling, fresh from the egg, attaches itself to the first moving object it sees. Usually that's the mother goose. In Lorenz's experiments... it was Lorenz. The goslings followed the bearded scientist around like devoted children, through adolescence, forever. One glimpse at the wrong moment — imprinted for life.
Ariely's claim tonight: your mind does the same thing with prices. The first number you ever associate with a thing becomes its mother — and you follow that number for years.
The pearls that were worthless on Tuesday and priceless on Friday

In the 1970s, a pearl dealer named Salvador Assael acquired something the market had never seen: black pearls, from Tahitian oysters. He took them to market... and failed completely. No one had ever priced a black pearl. No anchor, no interest. Gray-black spheres of dubious worth.

What Assael did next is the whole lesson. He did not discount. He persuaded the legendary jeweler Harry Winston to put the black pearls in his Fifth Avenue window — strung beside diamonds and rubies, priced extravagantly — and backed it with full-page ads in the glossiest magazines. Within a season, black pearls hung on the necks of Manhattan's wealthiest women.

Nothing about the pearls changed, friend. Only the first number attached to them. Imprinted beside diamonds, they were luxury forever — and every black pearl since has been priced against that anchor, not against any objective quality. Two refinements to carry: a number only imprints when you actually contemplate paying it — it must be a live reference, not a passing sight. And once set, anchors govern whole categories: the first flat-screen TV price you seriously considered calibrated every TV you've judged since.
The auction rigged by your own Social Security number

But surely, you say, the anchor must at least be plausible? Surely a random number couldn't move real bids? Ariely and two colleagues tested exactly that on MIT business students — wine, a cordless keyboard, chocolates on the auction block.

Pause and guess: Before bidding, each student simply wrote down the last two digits of their own Social Security number — pure noise — and answered "would you pay this much?" Then they bid for real. What did the meaningless digits do to the bids?
Ready? Here is what happened.

Students with high digits bid 60 to 120 percent more than students with low digits — for the same wine, the same keyboard. A number carrying zero information dominated a room of MIT's finest, merely because they had considered it as a possible price. And now the subtle part, the part that makes this invisible from inside: within each student's sheet, prices were perfectly sensible — everyone bid more for the better wine, more for the keyboard than the trackball. The relationships were coherent and logical; only the overall level was arbitrary, dragged up or down by noise. Ariely named it arbitrary coherence: first prices are arbitrary, but everything after stays consistent with them — so your pricing always feels rational, because it is internally consistent... even when its foundation is a lottery number. Defense: before any significant purchase, write your own maximum price down before you look at the tag — and treat every MSRP, "regular price," and crossed-out number as a planted anchor, never as information.
Tom Sawyer's fence

Anchors go deeper than levels, friend — they can decide whether an experience is a pleasure or a pain at all. Remember Tom Sawyer, sentenced to whitewash a fence, who reframed the chore as a rare privilege — and had the neighborhood boys paying him for a turn with the brush.

Ariely ran Twain's trick as science. He announced a short recital of Walt Whitman's poetry. One group of students was first asked: would you pay $10 to hear it? The other group: would you accept $10 to sit through it? Then both groups bid on tickets to short, medium, and long readings.

The single framing question flipped the market's sign. The pay-framed students offered money to attend — more for longer readings. The compensation-framed students demanded payment — more for longer readings. Identical recital; opposite prices; each group perfectly coherent within itself, scaling sensibly with duration from an arbitrary starting frame. Neither group had any way to know whether amateur Whitman was a treat or an ordeal — so the first cue settled it, forever. Now look around your own life: how many "preferences" — for networking events, exotic foods, kinds of work — rest not on the experience, but on whichever frame reached you first? When something new and ambiguous arrives, notice its packaging — and deliberately try the opposite frame before you price it.
Standing in line behind yourself

And here is the strangest anchor factory of all: you. Ordinary herding is inferring quality from a crowd — the queue outside the restaurant. Ariely's twist is self-herding: you observe your own past behavior and treat it as evidence — quietly lining up behind the earlier version of yourself.

The case study is in your hand most mornings. Once upon a time you walked into a Starbucks — tired, curious — and paid several dollars for a coffee that cost a fraction elsewhere. Next time you passed: I've done this before, so it must be worth it. By the fourth visit the deliberation was gone; a single questionable decision had compounded into a habit, the habit into an identity — I'm a Starbucks person — and the original judgment was never revisited. And Howard Schultz engineered the trap knowingly: Starbucks was styled as a continental coffeehouse — bean aroma, Italian names, Frappuccinos — so it felt categorically different from doughnut-shop coffee. No comparison available, no old anchor usable. Chapter one's relativity, deployed in reverse: instead of inviting comparison, Starbucks engineered its absence — leaving you open to a brand-new, much higher anchor, which you then reinforced with every visit, one vote at a time.
That is why first decisions deserve outsized care, friend: each repetition adds a vote to the internal record, making the behavior look ever more justified — regardless of whether it ever was.
The fallacy in the textbook

Now assemble the demolition. Standard economics says prices emerge where supply meets demand — and demand expresses consumers' true preferences. But if willingness to pay swings 60 to 120 percent on a Social Security number, doubles under a shop-window display, and flips sign on a single question... then demand is not an independent expression of anything. It is partly manufactured by the very market it supposedly disciplines. Suggested retail prices, advertised "regular" prices, first-mover pricing — all plant the anchors that later masquerade as consumer valuation. What we pay tracks historical anchors and marketing, not the pleasure we actually derive — a reason, Ariely argues, to view prices skeptically in domains like healthcare where anchors, not utility, may rule.

Your personal defense is the habit audit. Anchors reveal themselves in repetition — so interrogate your recurring purchases, not your one-offs. Trace the habit to its first decision: How did this start? Was that first choice reasoned — or planted? Would I start this habit today, at today's price? Anchors cannot be deleted, friend. But they can be dethroned — by consciously re-running the first decision with today's information. Pick one this week. Run the experiment.
Before you go

So: minds imprint on first prices like goslings on a scientist; arbitrary numbers produce coherent-feeling bids; a single question decides whether poetry is a treat or a chore; and your past self is quietly voting on your behalf every morning. The market's prices are less wise than they look — and so, until audited, are your habits.

But tomorrow, friend, we meet the strangest price of all — the one that breaks every rule anchoring taught us. A price that isn't just low, but magical. A price that makes rational people grab things they don't want, queue for hours, and switch away from better deals. Tomorrow: why Lindt truffles and Hershey kisses traded places the moment one number appeared — and what that number does to your brain.
Next time: the cost of zero — why FREE! makes us irrational.
Key takeaways
Minds imprint on first prices like goslings: the first number contemplated becomes the anchor for years.
Arbitrary coherence: bids swung 60–120% on Social Security digits, yet felt rational — consistency masks a random foundation.
Write your price before seeing the tag; treat MSRPs and crossed-out prices as planted anchors, not information.
Framing decides pleasure vs. pain: the same poetry earned or cost $10 depending on the first question — try the opposite frame.
Self-herding: each repetition votes for the habit — you may be standing in line behind an unexamined past self.
Run the habit audit: trace recurring spending to its first decision and re-decide with today's information.
